longTailKeywords: canal wall up vs canal wall down
_meta.authoringNotes: Definitions and surgical strategy per EAONO/JOS Joint Consensus on Definitions, Classifications and Staging of Middle Ear Cholesteatoma 2017 (Yung et al. PMID 28665449). Pediatric outcomes per Kemppainen Otol Neurotol 2011 and Sade & Berco classification. Non-echo-planar DWI MRI surveillance protocol per De Foer Otol Neurotol 2008 (PMID 18496474) and EAONO 2017. Hearing reconstruction outcomes per Iurato Otol Neurotol 2007. Epidemiology from Kemppainen 1999 PMID 10499099 and Tos 1988 (annual incidence 9 per 100,000). Facial nerve and CSF leak complication rates per Vincenti & Sanna J Laryngol Otol 2013. Congenital cholesteatoma per Potsic et al. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2002 (PMID 12162032).

h1Title: Cholesteatoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Cholesteatoma is an abnormal, expansile collection of keratinizing squamous epithelium within the middle ear or mastoid cavity that gradually erodes surrounding bone. Annual incidence in adults is roughly 9 per 100,000 (Tos 1988); pediatric incidence about 3 per 100,000, with bilateral disease in 10-15% of children. Patients present with chronic, foul-smelling ear discharge, progressive conductive hearing loss, and a defect or retraction pocket on the tympanic membrane visible at otoscopy. Untreated cholesteatoma erodes the ossicles in 70-80% of cases, can fistulize into the labyrinth or facial nerve canal, and rarely causes intracranial complications including meningitis, brain abscess, and lateral sinus thrombosis. The only definitive treatment is surgical removal (tympanomastoidectomy); recurrence rates of 5-25% in adults and 15-40% in children mandate long-term follow-up with non-echo-planar diffusion-weighted MRI or planned second-look surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence approximately 9 per 100,000 adults and 3 per 100,000 children (Tos 1988; Kemppainen 1999); cumulative lifetime prevalence about 0.1%
keyStats.demographics: Slight male predominance (1.4:1); peaks at age 5-15 (acquired pediatric) and 30-50 (acquired adult); congenital cholesteatoma typically detected age 2-10
keyStats.avgAge: Pediatric acquired: 8-12 years; adult acquired: 30-50; congenital: 4 years on average
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 1 in 1,000 ENT consultations is for cholesteatoma; significantly higher prevalence in low-resource regions with untreated chronic suppurative otitis media

definition: Cholesteatoma (ICD-10: H71) is a destructive epithelial cyst within the middle ear cleft (tympanic cavity, attic, antrum, mastoid air cells) lined by keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium. The lesion is not a true neoplasm but produces matrix expansion, periostitis, and bone erosion through release of osteolytic cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-alpha, RANKL) by the perimatrix. Cholesteatomas are classified by the EAONO/JOS 2017 consensus as congenital (white pearl behind an intact eardrum with no history of otorrhea or eardrum surgery) or acquired, with acquired forms subdivided into primary acquired (retraction-pocket-derived, most commonly Prussak's space or pars flaccida) and secondary acquired (following tympanic-membrane perforation, trauma, or post-surgical implantation). Stage is assigned by anatomic extent and reflects surgical complexity and prognosis: Stage I (limited to one site, attic or mesotympanum), Stage II (two or more sites), Stage III (extracranial complications such as labyrinthine fistula or facial nerve involvement), and Stage IV (intracranial complications). Cholesteatoma is managed exclusively by otolaryngology, with otology and neurotology subspecialists handling advanced disease, pediatric cases, and skull-base complications.

typesClassification.description: Most common form (60-70% of all cholesteatomas). Eustachian tube dysfunction creates persistent negative middle-ear pressure that retracts the tympanic membrane, most commonly at Prussak's space in the attic. Keratin accumulates in the retraction pocket and progresses to expansile disease.

Develops after tympanic membrane perforation, trauma, or otologic surgery. Squamous epithelium migrates inward from the external auditory canal margins through the perforation. Accounts for 15-25% of cases.

Present at birth from embryonic epithelial rests; presents in children age 2-10 as a white pearl behind an intact eardrum, classically in the anterior-superior quadrant. No prior otorrhea or eardrum surgery. Accounts for 2-5% of cholesteatomas.

Rare, often congenital cholesteatoma of the petrous portion of the temporal bone. Presents with facial palsy, retrobulbar pain, or sixth-nerve palsy from cavernous sinus involvement rather than otorrhea. Requires lateral or anterior petrosectomy.

Squamous epithelium growing on the external auditory canal wall, often with bone erosion (keratosis obturans variant). Can mimic neoplasm and requires biopsy if confused with malignant otitis externa or carcinoma.

primarySymptoms: Chronic, foul-smelling otorrhea that does not respond to repeated courses of antibiotics — the hallmark complaint, present in 60-80% of acquired cases.
Progressive conductive hearing loss of 20-40 dB caused by ossicular erosion (typically incus long process and stapes superstructure).
Retraction pocket or perforation in the pars flaccida or posterior-superior quadrant visible at otoscopy, often containing white keratin debris.
White pearl visible behind an intact tympanic membrane on otoscopy in congenital cholesteatoma, classically in the anterior-superior quadrant in children age 2-10.
Ear fullness, pressure, and intermittent dull pain in chronic disease; severe pain is uncommon and suggests complication.
Vertigo or imbalance, particularly with Valsalva maneuver or pressure changes — pneumatic-otoscopy-induced vertigo (positive fistula test) suggests labyrinthine fistula.
Facial weakness ranging from subtle asymmetry to House-Brackmann grade III-VI palsy when the lesion erodes the fallopian canal.
Tinnitus, often pulsatile when adjacent to the sigmoid sinus or due to ossicular fixation.
Headache, fever, photophobia, neck stiffness, or altered consciousness — red-flag features suggesting intracranial complication (meningitis, abscess, sinus thrombosis).
Discharge that recurs after each grommet (tympanostomy tube) placement in children should raise concern for underlying cholesteatoma.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent ear discharge despite appropriate antibiotic and aural toilet care
Persistent or deepening retraction pocket in the pars flaccida or posterior-superior quadrant on follow-up otoscopy
Conductive hearing loss greater than expected for a simple tympanic membrane perforation
White debris visible in a retraction pocket or behind an intact tympanic membrane
New tinnitus, episodic vertigo, or unilateral hearing loss in a patient with known chronic otitis media
emergencySigns: Acute facial nerve palsy in a patient with chronic ear disease — emergency surgical decompression within 24-48 hours
Fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, or altered mental status — exclude bacterial meningitis or intracranial abscess with urgent CT and lumbar puncture
New onset vertigo with sensorineural hearing loss — labyrinthine fistula requires urgent imaging and surgical assessment
Postauricular swelling, fluctuance, or fistula — Bezold abscess, subperiosteal abscess, or mastoiditis requires drainage and intravenous antibiotics
Seizure, focal neurological deficit, or papilledema in a patient with chronic otorrhea — exclude lateral sinus thrombosis or brain abscess with contrast MRI

causes.description: Persistent negative middle-ear pressure from poor eustachian tube ventilation pulls the pars flaccida or posterior quadrant of the tympanic membrane into the attic, creating a retraction pocket. The pocket cannot self-clean and accumulates keratin that progressively expands. This is the dominant mechanism in 60-70% of acquired cases.

Recurrent acute otitis media and chronic otitis media with effusion in childhood damage the middle-ear mucosa, weaken the tympanic membrane, and predispose to retraction. Cleft palate and craniofacial syndromes confer especially high lifetime risk.

Squamous epithelium of the external canal migrates inward through a marginal perforation (especially posterior-superior) to colonize the middle ear in secondary acquired cholesteatoma. Persistent perforations after acute otitis media or trauma should be repaired to prevent this complication.

Congenital cholesteatomas arise from epithelial rests trapped in the middle ear during fetal development, classically in the anterior-superior quadrant near the eustachian tube orifice (Michaels 1986 hypothesis). They present in children 2-10 years with no history of otorrhea.

Squamous epithelium can be implanted into the middle ear during tympanostomy tube insertion, tympanoplasty, or mastoidectomy if technique is suboptimal. Iatrogenic cholesteatoma typically presents 2-10 years post-procedure and is often well-defined.

Family history of cholesteatoma raises personal risk 5-10 fold; sibling and parent-child concordance suggest a multifactorial heritable component. Bilateral disease in children, in particular, warrants assessment of craniofacial anatomy and family screening.

riskFactors.factor: Recurrent acute otitis media in childhood
riskFactors.description: Three or more episodes of acute otitis media in 12 months doubles cholesteatoma risk over a lifetime. Prompt antibiotic treatment and tympanostomy tubes in children with persistent middle-ear effusion reduce long-term sequelae.

Chronic eustachian tube dysfunction underlies most retraction-pocket cholesteatomas. Cleft palate raises lifetime risk approximately 7-fold; Down syndrome and other craniofacial anomalies are similarly affected.

Cleft palate confers a 7-9 fold higher lifetime cholesteatoma risk through eustachian tube dysfunction. Aggressive otologic surveillance and early grommet placement are recommended in affected children.

riskFactors.factor: Previous tympanic membrane perforation or otologic surgery
riskFactors.description: Marginal or large perforations and previous tympanostomy tube placement increase secondary acquired cholesteatoma risk. Tympanoplasty for symptomatic perforations is recommended in adolescents and adults.
Sibling or parent with cholesteatoma increases personal risk 5-10 fold. Assessment of first-degree relatives is recommended in pediatric and adolescent cases, particularly bilateral disease.
Approximately 1.4 male-to-female prevalence ratio in most series. Reasons unclear; possibly related to eustachian tube anatomy and childhood otitis media patterns.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and limited healthcare access
riskFactors.description: Untreated chronic suppurative otitis media in low-resource settings increases secondary cholesteatoma incidence substantially. WHO estimates 65-330 million people globally have chronic suppurative otitis media.

affectedDemographics: United States: estimated 30,000-40,000 new cases per year across all ages.
Pediatric patients: account for 20-30% of all cholesteatomas; congenital cases peak around age 4, acquired pediatric cases around age 8-12.
Patients with cleft palate: 5-15% develop cholesteatoma by adolescence despite optimal otologic care.
Low- and middle-income countries: significantly higher incidence due to untreated chronic suppurative otitis media; data limited but estimated 2-5 times the adult incidence in high-income countries.
Patients with Down syndrome: lifetime cholesteatoma risk approximately 3-7 times that of the general population.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical otoscopic examination with audiometry and selected imaging. Microsuction with a binocular operating microscope or oto-endoscope is the cornerstone of clinical assessment: removal of keratin debris and granulation tissue exposes the retraction pocket, perforation, or pearl. Documented findings include site (pars flaccida attic, pars tensa, posterior-superior, anterior-superior), extent (limited or extensive), bone erosion, and any visible middle-ear structures (ossicles, facial canal). Pure-tone audiometry quantifies conductive, mixed, or rarely sensorineural hearing loss and informs ossicular reconstruction planning. Imaging is required preoperatively for all cases. Non-contrast high-resolution CT of the temporal bones (0.6 mm slices) maps anatomy: tegmen tympani thickness, sigmoid sinus position, facial canal integrity, scutum erosion, ossicular continuity, lateral semicircular canal fistula, and mastoid pneumatization. CT identifies bony erosion patterns but cannot reliably distinguish granulation tissue from cholesteatoma matrix. Non-echo-planar diffusion-weighted MRI is the modern standard for distinguishing cholesteatoma from inflammation: cholesteatomas show restricted diffusion (bright on b1000) due to densely packed keratin, with sensitivity 90% and specificity 95% for lesions over 3 mm (De Foer 2008). This imaging has reduced the need for routine second-look surgery in many centers. Pneumatic otoscopy and the fistula test (positive vertigo or nystagmus with pressure changes) suggest labyrinthine fistula. Facial nerve function is documented with House-Brackmann grading. In children, examination under anesthesia may be required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of retraction pocket, perforation, keratin debris, and middle-ear structures after clearance of obstructing material
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minute office procedure using a binocular operating microscope or rigid endoscope; mild discomfort during suction. Photo-documentation is standard.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies conductive, mixed, or sensorineural hearing loss; informs ossicular reconstruction planning and counseling on outcome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute test in a sound-treated booth. Results immediately available; the audiologist reviews the audiogram with you.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps bony anatomy: tegmen thickness, sigmoid sinus position, facial canal integrity, scutum erosion, ossicular continuity, lateral canal fistula
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-10 minute scan in the radiology department; thin-slice protocols preferred. Provides surgical roadmap but cannot distinguish cholesteatoma from granulation tissue alone.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes cholesteatoma matrix (restricted diffusion, bright on b1000) from granulation tissue with 90% sensitivity, 95% specificity for lesions over 3 mm
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-30 minutes inside the MRI scanner. No contrast required. Useful for postoperative surveillance to avoid routine second-look surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses tympanic membrane mobility and middle-ear pressure; type B or C tracings support effusion or retraction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-second probe-tip test per ear. No discomfort beyond brief pressure change.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provokes pressure-induced vertigo or nystagmus when a labyrinthine fistula is present
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Pneumatic otoscope or Politzer balloon applies pressure to the ear canal for a few seconds. Positive test prompts urgent surgical planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Permits adequate microsuction and assessment in children unable to tolerate office examination
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case general anesthesia; combined diagnostic and minor therapeutic clearance often performed.

treatmentOverview: The only definitive treatment for cholesteatoma is complete surgical removal. The objectives, in order of priority, are: produce a safe, dry, disease-free ear, preserve or restore hearing, and prevent recurrence. Two main surgical approaches are used. Canal-wall-up (CWU) tympanomastoidectomy preserves the external auditory canal posterior wall, maintains normal ear anatomy, and supports better hearing reconstruction but carries 20-40% recurrence rate at 5 years, particularly in cholesteatomas with extensive attic and mastoid involvement. Canal-wall-down (CWD) mastoidectomy removes the posterior canal wall, producing a single open mastoid cavity that can be visualized in clinic; recurrence rates fall to 5-15%, but patients require lifelong cavity care and water precautions, and ear-canal infections are more common. Modified CWD with reconstruction (obliteration with bone pâté, cartilage, or fascia) combines the lower recurrence of CWD with better cavity hygiene. Endoscopic ear surgery, increasingly adopted, achieves complete removal of limited attic and mesotympanic disease through a transcanal approach without mastoidectomy in 30-50% of selected adult cases. Ossicular reconstruction (with autologous incus interposition, PORP/TORP titanium prostheses, or cartilage) is performed at the primary procedure or at a planned second-look 6-12 months later. Pediatric cholesteatoma recurrence rates (15-40%) mandate either planned second-look surgery or non-EPI DWI surveillance at 12 and 24 months. Antibiotic-steroid ear drops (ciprofloxacin-dexamethasone) control preoperative discharge but do not cure the underlying disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical ciprofloxacin-dexamethasone ear drops
medicalTreatments.description: Preoperative aural toilet to reduce discharge and granulation tissue, improving surgical visibility. Used 3-4 drops twice daily for 2-4 weeks. Does not cure cholesteatoma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces otorrhea in 60-80% of patients before surgery; relapse universal without surgical removal

medicalTreatments.name: Office microsuction with aural toilet
medicalTreatments.description: Regular cleaning of keratin debris and granulation tissue under a binocular microscope. Provides symptom control and disease monitoring while awaiting surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves discharge in 50-70% short-term; does not prevent progression

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotics for superimposed infection
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for systemic illness, acute on chronic infection, or complications. Anti-pseudomonal coverage (ciprofloxacin) appropriate when culture confirms Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls acute infection in 80-90% but does not eradicate cholesteatoma matrix

surgicalOptions.name: Canal-wall-up (CWU) tympanomastoidectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Cortical mastoidectomy and posterior tympanotomy with preservation of the posterior canal wall. Allows complete removal of disease with retained normal ear anatomy and superior hearing reconstruction potential.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Disease-free ear in 60-80% at 5 years; recurrence 20-40% in extensive disease; 5-15 dB conductive hearing gain after ossiculoplasty

surgicalOptions.name: Canal-wall-down (CWD) mastoidectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the posterior canal wall to create a single open mastoid cavity allowing visualization in clinic. Indicated for extensive disease, only-hearing ear, recurrent disease, or labyrinthine fistula.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Disease-free ear in 85-95% at 5 years; recurrence 5-15%; lifelong cavity care required; hearing reconstruction more challenging

surgicalOptions.name: Modified CWD with mastoid obliteration
surgicalOptions.description: Posterior wall removal combined with obliteration of the cavity using cartilage, bone pâté, hydroxyapatite, or fat. Combines low recurrence with reduced cavity-care burden.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Disease-free ear in 85-95% with smaller residual cavity; selected patients return to swimming with caution

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic transcanal ear surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Transcanal removal of attic, mesotympanic, and selected antrum disease using rigid endoscopes (0°, 30°, 45°). Avoids postauricular incision and mastoidectomy in 30-50% of carefully selected adult cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete removal in selected disease in 80-95%; recurrence 10-20% at 3-5 years in published series

surgicalOptions.name: Ossicular reconstruction (ossiculoplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Restoration of ossicular chain continuity with autologous incus interposition, PORP/TORP titanium prosthesis, or cartilage interposition. Performed at primary surgery or at second-look 6-12 months later.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Air-bone gap below 20 dB in 50-70% with PORP, 30-50% with TORP at 1 year (Iurato 2007)

surgicalOptions.name: Facial nerve decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for facial palsy resulting from cholesteatoma erosion of the fallopian canal. Removal of disease and decompression of the nerve preserves or restores function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: House-Brackmann grade I-II recovery in 70-85% when decompression performed within 2 weeks of palsy onset

whySeeSpecialist: Cholesteatoma requires evaluation by an otologist or general otolaryngologist with cholesteatoma surgical volume. The diagnosis is made in clinic but management is operative; delay risks ossicular destruction, hearing loss, facial nerve injury, and intracranial complications. Pediatric cases benefit from a pediatric otolaryngology service. Recurrent or extensive disease, only-hearing ear, and skull-base involvement need a neurotologist at a tertiary center.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an otologist performing at least 40 mastoidectomy and tympanoplasty procedures per year, with access to non-EPI DWI MRI surveillance and high-resolution temporal bone CT. Pediatric cases benefit from surgeons trained in both pediatric and otologic surgery. Endoscopic ear surgery experience is desirable for limited attic disease. Multidisciplinary cleft and craniofacial teams improve outcomes in patients with cleft palate.
hospitalCriteria: Otology and neurotology service with cholesteatoma volume above 100 cases per year
Pediatric otolaryngology team for paediatric cases
High-resolution temporal bone CT with 0.6 mm slices
Audiology service for pre- and post-operative testing
keyFacilities: Hearing rehabilitation services including bone-conduction implants
preventionStrategies: Treat acute otitis media promptly and ensure full resolution; address chronic middle-ear effusion with tympanostomy tubes when indicated.
Repair persistent or large tympanic membrane perforations in adolescents and adults with tympanoplasty.
Monitor children with cleft palate, Down syndrome, and other craniofacial anomalies with periodic otoscopy and audiometry from age 1 onward.
Avoid unsupervised cotton-bud or instrumental cleaning of the ear canal, which can perforate the eardrum and seed squamous epithelium.
Implement universal newborn hearing screening and follow-up audiology to detect early conductive losses indicating eustachian-tube dysfunction.
Ensure timely otolaryngology referral for any retraction pocket noted on routine otoscopy.
lifestyleModifications: Keep the ear dry during showering and washing — use a vaseline-coated cotton plug or commercial earplugs.
Avoid swimming and diving in untreated water; chlorinated pool swimming after surgery requires surgeon clearance.
Do not insert cotton buds, hairpins, or other objects into the ear canal.
Use prescribed ear drops as directed and complete the full course.
Attend regular postoperative follow-up — recurrent cholesteatoma is detected most reliably at scheduled review or non-EPI DWI scanning.
Treat upper respiratory infections promptly to reduce eustachian tube dysfunction episodes.

recommended: Balanced diet supporting general immune function during recovery
Adequate hydration (1.5-2 L water daily) particularly after surgery
Iron-rich foods if surgical blood loss has caused mild anemia
Adequate protein intake (1-1.2 g/kg/day) during postoperative recovery
avoid: Tobacco smoking — impairs middle-ear mucosa and wound healing
Heavy alcohol intake during postoperative recovery
Allergens that trigger upper respiratory inflammation in known atopic patients
Decongestant-only ear-drying remedies marketed online with unverified ingredients

exerciseGuidelines: Resume light activity within 1-2 weeks of surgery. Avoid heavy lifting, straining, and contact sports for 4-6 weeks while the graft heals. Air travel is generally allowed after 2-4 weeks at the surgeon's discretion. Scuba diving requires individualized assessment by an otologist — most patients with prior cholesteatoma are advised against deep diving because of barotrauma risk and the presence of a mastoid cavity.
dailyManagement: Inspect the ear for discharge or unusual smell; report changes
Apply prescribed ear drops at the right angle to reach the middle ear
Keep the ear dry until cleared by the surgeon
Use a hearing aid or bone-conduction device when prescribed for residual loss
Attend cavity-care appointments every 6-12 months in canal-wall-down patients
Have non-EPI DWI MRI at 12 and 24 months after canal-wall-up surgery
prognosis: Complete surgical removal at the primary procedure leads to a disease-free, dry ear in 60-85% of adult patients and 50-75% of pediatric patients at 5 years. Hearing outcomes depend on ossicular status at time of surgery and reconstruction method: closure of the air-bone gap to within 20 dB is achieved in 50-70% of cases with PORP and 30-50% with TORP. Recurrence rates differ by approach: 20-40% with canal-wall-up versus 5-15% with canal-wall-down; pediatric recurrence rates are higher because of faster epithelial growth and more aggressive disease. Facial palsy resolves to House-Brackmann grade I-II in 70-85% of cases when surgery occurs within 2 weeks of onset. Intracranial complications occur in under 1% of treated cases in high-income countries but remain substantially more common in low-resource settings with untreated disease. Lifelong follow-up is required because recurrent or residual disease can present years after apparently complete surgery.
recoveryTimeline: Postoperative discharge usually next day for simple tympanoplasty, 1-2 days after mastoidectomy. Outer dressing removal and gentle ear cleaning at 2 weeks. Audiogram at 6-8 weeks. Graft take confirmed at 6 weeks. Hearing improvement evident from 8-12 weeks and stabilizes by 6 months. Canal-wall-down cavity-care visits every 6-12 months; canal-wall-up DWI surveillance at 12 and 24 months. Second-look ossiculoplasty, when planned, performed 6-12 months after primary surgery.
complications: Hearing loss — conductive loss from ossicular erosion or sensorineural loss from labyrinthine fistula or surgical injury (sensorineural injury below 5% in modern series).
Facial nerve palsy — preoperative from disease (1-2%), intraoperative iatrogenic injury (under 1% with monitoring).
Labyrinthine fistula at the lateral semicircular canal in 5-10% of advanced cases; risks sensorineural hearing loss and vertigo.
Intracranial complications: meningitis, brain abscess, epidural abscess, lateral sinus thrombosis, otitic hydrocephalus — combined incidence 1-5% in advanced or untreated disease.
Cholesteatoma recurrence — 20-40% with canal-wall-up, 5-15% with canal-wall-down; pediatric rates higher.
Cavity problems: chronic discharge, granulation, deep cleaning needs, water restrictions, and hearing aid fitting difficulty in canal-wall-down ears.
Failure of ossicular reconstruction with persistent conductive hearing loss in 30-50% of cases requiring revision or hearing aid use.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery — Cholesteatoma
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on diagnosis and treatment from the leading US otolaryngology society.

supportResources.name: EAONO (European Academy of Otology and Neurotology)
supportResources.description: European society publishing the EAONO/JOS Joint Consensus on Cholesteatoma 2017 and guidelines for surgeons and patients.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cholesteatoma
supportResources.description: Plain-language UK National Health Service resource for patients and families.

supportResources.name: ENT UK — Patient Information on Mastoid Surgery
supportResources.description: UK-based patient leaflets on cholesteatoma and mastoid surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media (CSOM)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CSOM produces persistent perforation and discharge from middle-ear mucosal inflammation without keratinizing squamous epithelium in the middle ear. Otoscopy shows pulsatile mucoid discharge through a central perforation; cholesteatoma shows white keratin debris in a retraction pocket or behind an intact membrane, with characteristic bone erosion on CT and restricted diffusion on MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Age-related Hearing Loss (Presbycusis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Presbycusis produces bilateral, symmetric high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss without discharge or otoscopic abnormality. Cholesteatoma causes unilateral conductive hearing loss with chronic foul-smelling discharge and a retraction pocket or keratin pearl on otoscopy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acoustic Neuroma (Vestibular Schwannoma)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vestibular schwannoma causes asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss and tinnitus with a normal eardrum and an enhancing internal acoustic meatus lesion on MRI. Cholesteatoma causes conductive hearing loss with otoscopic abnormality and bone erosion on CT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malignant Otitis Externa
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malignant otitis externa is an aggressive Pseudomonas infection of the external ear canal and skull base in immunocompromised adults, presenting with severe pain, granulation in the canal, and cranial nerve palsies. Cholesteatoma typically lacks severe pain and shows keratin in a middle-ear pocket on otoscopy.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media
relatedConditions.relevance: Common precursor or coexisting condition; cholesteatoma may complicate long-standing CSOM with marginal perforation.

relatedConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
relatedConditions.relevance: May develop from labyrinthine fistula, surgical injury, or co-existing presbycusis after long-standing cholesteatoma.

relatedConditions.name: Tinnitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Common symptom of cholesteatoma-related hearing loss and after surgical reconstruction.

relatedConditions.name: Acoustic Neuroma
relatedConditions.relevance: Alternative ENT cause of unilateral hearing loss; both require MRI evaluation though imaging features differ.

faqs.question: What is cholesteatoma in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Cholesteatoma is an abnormal skin-like growth in the middle ear that gradually erodes the small bones of hearing and surrounding structures. It usually develops from a retraction pocket caused by long-standing eustachian tube dysfunction. The only effective treatment is surgical removal.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of cholesteatoma?
faqs.answer: Typical symptoms include chronic, foul-smelling ear discharge that does not respond to antibiotics, gradually worsening hearing in the affected ear, ear fullness, and occasional dizziness. Severe pain, facial weakness, severe headache, or fever indicate complications requiring urgent assessment.

faqs.question: Is cholesteatoma a cancer?
faqs.answer: No. Cholesteatoma is not a cancer or malignant tumor. It is an abnormal but benign collection of skin cells inside the middle ear. However, it grows progressively, destroys nearby bone, and can cause serious complications if left untreated.

faqs.question: How is cholesteatoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by an ENT specialist using a binocular microscope or endoscope to inspect the ear canal and middle ear. Hearing tests, high-resolution CT of the temporal bones, and non-echo-planar diffusion-weighted MRI confirm the diagnosis, map anatomy, and guide surgical planning.

faqs.question: Can cholesteatoma be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: No. Surgery is the only definitive treatment for cholesteatoma. Antibiotic ear drops and microsuction provide temporary relief from discharge but do not eradicate the disease. Untreated cholesteatoma progresses and can cause serious complications including hearing loss and meningitis.

faqs.question: What is tympanomastoidectomy?
faqs.answer: Tympanomastoidectomy is the surgical removal of cholesteatoma combined with mastoidectomy and repair of the eardrum. It can be performed canal-wall-up (preserving normal anatomy) or canal-wall-down (creating an open cavity), depending on disease extent and recurrence risk.

faqs.question: What is the difference between canal-wall-up and canal-wall-down surgery?
faqs.answer: Canal-wall-up preserves the natural ear canal wall, maintains anatomy and better hearing potential but has higher recurrence (20-40%). Canal-wall-down removes the wall, creating an open mastoid cavity with lower recurrence (5-15%) but requires lifelong cavity care and water precautions.

faqs.question: Can cholesteatoma come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrent or residual disease occurs in 20-40% of canal-wall-up procedures and 5-15% of canal-wall-down procedures. Pediatric rates are higher. Surveillance with non-EPI diffusion-weighted MRI at 12 and 24 months or planned second-look surgery detects recurrence early.

faqs.question: Will my hearing improve after cholesteatoma surgery?
faqs.answer: Hearing improvement depends on ossicular damage at the time of surgery. With ossicular reconstruction, 50-70% of patients achieve an air-bone gap within 20 dB at 1 year. Severely damaged ossicles may need a TORP prosthesis or bone-conduction hearing aid. Some patients still require amplification afterwards.

faqs.question: Can cholesteatoma cause facial paralysis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Erosion of the facial nerve canal by the cholesteatoma can produce partial or complete facial weakness in 1-2% of cases. This requires urgent surgical removal and decompression. Recovery to grade I-II House-Brackmann function occurs in 70-85% when treated within 2 weeks of onset.

faqs.question: Are children at higher risk of cholesteatoma?
faqs.answer: Children with recurrent ear infections, cleft palate, Down syndrome, and other craniofacial anomalies have higher risk. Pediatric cholesteatoma also tends to grow faster and recur more often than adult disease, with 15-40% recurrence after surgery. Bilateral disease occurs in 10-15% of children.

faqs.question: What is congenital cholesteatoma?
faqs.answer: Congenital cholesteatoma is present from birth and arises from embryonic epithelial rests trapped in the middle ear. It is typically detected age 2-10 as a white pearl behind an intact eardrum, classically in the anterior-superior quadrant. It accounts for 2-5% of cases.

faqs.question: Can cholesteatoma cause meningitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Erosion of the tegmen tympani allows infection to spread intracranially, causing meningitis, brain abscess, or lateral sinus thrombosis in 1-5% of advanced disease. Symptoms include fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, and altered consciousness — these require emergency assessment.

faqs.question: How long does cholesteatoma surgery take?
faqs.answer: Tympanomastoidectomy typically takes 2-4 hours under general anesthesia, depending on disease extent. Endoscopic transcanal surgery for limited disease may be shorter (1-2 hours). Most adults go home the same day or the next morning; children may stay overnight for observation.

faqs.question: Can I fly after cholesteatoma surgery?
faqs.answer: Most surgeons allow short-haul flights at 2-4 weeks after surgery and long-haul flights at 4-6 weeks, once the eardrum graft has healed. Pressure changes during ascent and descent can disturb early healing. Always confirm with your surgical team before booking travel.

faqs.question: Is endoscopic ear surgery suitable for cholesteatoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Endoscopic ear surgery is increasingly used for limited attic and mesotympanic disease, avoiding the postauricular incision and mastoidectomy in 30-50% of carefully selected adult cases. Results are comparable to microscopic surgery in suitable patients.

faqs.question: Can I swim with a cholesteatoma?
faqs.answer: Swimming with active cholesteatoma is discouraged because water entry worsens discharge and infection. After canal-wall-down surgery, water precautions are lifelong unless the cavity is fully epithelialized. Custom swim plugs can allow chlorinated pool swimming in selected patients.

faqs.question: What is non-EPI diffusion-weighted MRI?
faqs.answer: Non-echo-planar diffusion-weighted MRI is a specialized scan that distinguishes cholesteatoma matrix from inflammation by detecting restricted water diffusion in densely packed keratin. Sensitivity is 90% and specificity 95% for lesions over 3 mm, and it can replace routine second-look surgery in many cases.

faqs.question: Does cholesteatoma run in families?
faqs.answer: Family history raises personal risk approximately 5-10 fold. A multifactorial heritable component combines with eustachian tube anatomy and childhood infection patterns. Siblings of pediatric cases, especially bilateral disease, warrant otoscopy and audiometry to screen for early disease.

faqs.question: Can cholesteatoma be prevented?
faqs.answer: Some risk factors are modifiable. Prompt treatment of acute otitis media, addressing chronic middle-ear effusion with grommets, repairing persistent eardrum perforations, monitoring children with cleft palate and Down syndrome, and avoiding cotton-bud cleaning of ears reduce risk.

faqs.question: How long does follow-up last after cholesteatoma surgery?
faqs.answer: Follow-up is essentially lifelong because recurrent or residual disease can appear years after apparently successful surgery. Canal-wall-up patients have non-EPI DWI scans at 12 and 24 months; canal-wall-down patients have cavity-care appointments every 6-12 months indefinitely.

metaDescription: Cholesteatoma: middle-ear skin cyst causing hearing loss, foul discharge. Symptoms, mastoidectomy surgery, recurrence. Medically reviewed ENT guide.
